---

## FAN Research Plan — pl_003
**Question q_001:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland; d. 1908, Schuylkill County, PA)?

**Why a new plan:** pl_002 exhausted the direct census/vital/probate route. The proof stands at *Probable* — Thomas Flynn is named on the 1850 and 1860 censuses (indirect, no relationship column) and on the death certificate (secondary informant). A FAN pivot now targets records of Patrick's family, associates, and neighbors to add independent corroboration and identify the unknown mother.

---

### Execution Order

| # | ID | Record Type | What it targets | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | pli_007 | Census 1870 | **Patrick Flynn**, Schuylkill Co. | Fills 48-year gap; reveals Patrick's household, spouse, children, and Irish-community neighbors — the FAN cluster |
| 2 | pli_008 | Census 1880 | **Patrick Flynn**, Schuylkill Co. | First census with explicit relationship column; parents' birthplace column corroborates Thomas Flynn's Irish origins |
| 3 | pli_009 | Census 1870 | **Thomas Flynn**, Schuylkill Co. | Identifies Patrick's siblings (prime FAN targets) still in Thomas's household; if Thomas is absent, narrows his death window |
| 4 | pli_010 | Church records | **Thomas Flynn's marriage**, Schuylkill Co. parishes 1840–1870 (FS coll. 1401638) | Mother's maiden name — the single most important missing fact; baptism records would directly name both parents |
| 5 | pli_011 | Vital record | **Patrick Flynn's marriage**, Pennsylvania civil/county marriages (FS colls. 1571957, 1921317) | Catholic ceremony records often name "son of Thomas Flynn and [wife]" — direct parentage evidence from Patrick himself |
| 6 | pli_012 | Vital record | **Patrick's siblings' death certificates** post-1906 (FS coll. 2255317) | Siblings' death certs independently name Thomas Flynn + wife; multiple children naming the same parents is strong GPS corroboration |
| 7 | pli_013 | Court/naturalization | **Thomas Flynn naturalization**, Schuylkill Co. Court 1845–1880 (Ancestry) | Declarations of intent name birthplace, age, sometimes family; distinguishes this Thomas from other county Flynns |
| 8 | pli_014 | Newspaper | **Patrick Flynn obituary**, Schuylkill Co. papers March–April 1908 (Newspapers.com, GenealogyBank, Chronicling America) | Obituaries of the era named siblings, surviving family, and often Irish place of origin |

---

### Fallback Relationships
Items 3 → 6 are **sequential dependencies**: finding Thomas Flynn in 1870 (pli_009) names the siblings whose death certificates are the target of pli_012. Run them in order.

Items 1 and 2 are independent and can run in parallel with item 3.

### Scope
8 searches across FamilySearch (free, items 1–6), Ancestry (item 7), and newspaper repositories (item 8).

---

**Ready to start?** Say "execute the plan" and I'll begin with pli_007 (Patrick Flynn 1870 census on FamilySearch) and pli_009 (Thomas Flynn 1870 census) in parallel.

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn has not been located in any census after 1860 — a 48-year gap before his 1908 death. The 1870 census (Patrick age ~25) bridges that gap and reveals his new household, spouse, children, and — critically — his neighbors. Irish immigrant communities in Pennsylvania's coal region were tight-knit; Patrick's 1870 neighbors are likely from the same Schuylkill County Irish cluster as Thomas Flynn, and identifying them is the starting point for the FAN pivot. Any co-resident relatives (e.g., a sibling) would be direct FAN evidence linking Patrick to the Flynn family.

The 1880 census is the first U.S. census to record stated relationships (not just inferred from household position). If Patrick has a sibling or parent living nearby or in his household, the relationship column provides direct evidence. The 1880 census also records parents' birthplace — for each of Patrick's children, the enumerator recorded the birthplaces of both parents; consistent entries of 'Ireland' for Patrick's father corroborate Thomas Flynn's Irish origins. Neighbors in 1880 who also appear in the 1850/1860 Thomas Flynn neighborhood are prime FAN targets.

Thomas Flynn last appeared in the 1860 census (dwelling 112). The 1870 census establishes whether he survived past 1860 and, if so, who remained in his household. Any children still living with Thomas in 1870 are Patrick's siblings — and their names, birth years, and birthplaces (Ireland or Pennsylvania) are the key inputs for searching their own later death certificates (pli_012), which would independently name Thomas Flynn and his wife as parents. If Thomas is absent in 1870, it narrows his death window to 1860–1870 and explains why probate was not found.

The Flynn family was almost certainly Irish Catholic — the surname, Irish birth, and Pennsylvania coal region community all point to this. FamilySearch collection 1401638 (Pennsylvania Church and Town Records, 1708–1985, 1.2M records) indexes Catholic parish registers including St. Patrick's Church (Pottsville) and other Schuylkill County parishes active from the 1840s. A marriage record for Thomas Flynn would supply his wife's maiden name — the single most important missing fact in this investigation, absent from every record searched so far. Baptism records for the Flynn children would directly name Thomas Flynn and his wife as parents, providing primary-quality evidence of parentage. Burial records would establish Thomas Flynn's death date.

Patrick Flynn's marriage record is a high-value FAN document. Pennsylvania Catholic marriage ceremonies of this period routinely recorded the groom's parents' names (sometimes including the mother's maiden name) and occasionally the groom's specific townland of origin in Ireland. FamilySearch collections 1571957 (Pennsylvania Civil Marriages, 1677–1950) and 1921317 (Pennsylvania County Marriages, 1885–1950) cover Schuylkill County. If Patrick married before 1885 the civil collections may not apply, but a church register (covered by collection 1401638 above) would. A marriage record naming Patrick's parents — 'son of Thomas Flynn and [wife]' — would be direct evidence of parentage from a record created close to the time and by a knowledgeable informant (Patrick himself).

Patrick's siblings are the most productive FAN target. The 1850 census (dwelling 84) and 1860 census (dwelling 112) each listed Patrick in a Thomas Flynn household alongside other family members. The 1870 Thomas Flynn census search (pli_009) will identify surviving siblings by name and birth year. Any sibling who died in or after 1906 would have a Pennsylvania death certificate (FamilySearch collection 2255317, 4.2M records). A sibling's death certificate naming Thomas Flynn as their father — ideally with the mother's name — independently confirms Thomas Flynn's parentage from a second child's record, satisfying the GPS corroboration requirement. Multiple siblings' certificates naming the same parents constitute strong cumulative evidence.

Thomas Flynn was Irish-born and would have needed to naturalize to vote and to hold certain property rights. The Schuylkill County Court of Common Pleas handled naturalizations, and its records are indexed on Ancestry (Pennsylvania, U.S., Naturalization Records). A Declaration of Intention (first papers) typically names the declarant's birthplace, age, and sometimes wife and children. If Thomas declared intent alongside Patrick — or if Patrick naturalized separately and named his father — the naturalization file corroborates both men's Irish origins and the family relationship. This is also a check on Thomas Flynn's identity: naturalization papers can distinguish him from other Thomas Flynns in the county.

Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County. Schuylkill County newspapers of the era — particularly the Miners' Journal (Pottsville) and the Shenandoah Evening Herald — routinely published obituaries naming the deceased's surviving spouse, children, and siblings, and often mentioned the deceased's place of birth in Ireland. A 1908 obituary for Patrick Flynn naming his siblings would identify additional FAN members; a mention of his parents (even 'son of the late Thomas Flynn') would add a community-knowledge layer of corroboration. Search Newspapers.com, GenealogyBank, and Chronicling America (LOC) for Schuylkill County papers in March–April 1908.

---

## Research Plan — pl_003
**Question:** q_001 — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland; d. 1908, Schuylkill County, PA)?
**Context:** pl_002 is completed (1860 census ✓, death certificate ✓, probate — negative). The parentage conclusion sits at *Probable*. The 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses are the primary remaining gap.
**Note:** The 1890 census is excluded — Pennsylvania population schedules were largely destroyed in the 1921 Commerce Department fire.

---

### Execution Order

| # | ID | Year | Jurisdiction | Repository | Fallback for |
|---|-----|------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | pli_007 | 1870 | Schuylkill County, PA | FamilySearch | — |
| 2 | pli_008 | 1870 | Schuylkill County, PA | Ancestry | pli_007 |
| 3 | **pli_009** | **1880** | **Schuylkill County, PA** | **FamilySearch** | — |
| 4 | pli_010 | 1880 | Schuylkill County, PA | Ancestry | pli_009 |
| 5 | pli_011 | 1900 | Schuylkill County, PA | FamilySearch | — |
| 6 | pli_012 | 1900 | Schuylkill County, PA | Ancestry | pli_011 |
| 7 | pli_013 | 1870–1880 | Pennsylvania statewide | FamilySearch | contingency |

---

### Item Rationales

**pli_007 — 1870 Census, FamilySearch** *(start here)*
Patrick (~25) may still share a dwelling with Thomas (~52), or may have just established his own household. Search Patrick first, note household composition, then search Thomas independently. Co-residence in 1870 adds a third indirect data point after 1850 and 1860; separate households is equally informative for dating Patrick's household formation. FamilySearch indexes this census freely (NARA M593).

**pli_008 — 1870 Census, Ancestry** *(fallback if pli_007 finds nothing)*
Ancestry's independent indexing catches name variants ("Flinn," "Flyn") and may correct transcription errors from FamilySearch's pass.

**pli_009 — 1880 Census, FamilySearch** ⭐ *highest-priority item in the plan*
The 1880 census was the **first federal census to explicitly state each person's relationship to the head of household.** If Patrick (~35) and Thomas (~62) share an 1880 household, the relationship column provides **direct evidence of parentage** — a decisive upgrade from the indirect, inferred co-residence evidence of 1850 and 1860, and from the secondary-informant death certificate. This single record, if found, could advance the proof summary from *Probable* to *Proved*. Even if they are in separate households, finding both individuals narrows Thomas's death window considerably.

**pli_010 — 1880 Census, Ancestry** *(fallback if pli_009 finds nothing)*
Given the 1880 census's unique evidentiary weight (relationship column), exhausting both major indexes before concluding absence is warranted.

**pli_011 — 1900 Census, FamilySearch**
Patrick (~55) is the focus; Thomas would be ~82 — possible but unlikely. If Thomas is alive and living with Patrick, the 1900 relationship column confirms the link directly. Even if Thomas has died, the 1900 record fills the identity timeline and captures birth month/year, years married, and children — data that corroborates Patrick's single identity across records.

**pli_012 — 1900 Census, Ancestry** *(fallback if pli_011 finds nothing)*
Alternate index with name-variant matching.

**pli_013 — Pennsylvania statewide, 1870–1880, FamilySearch** *(contingency only)*
Execute **only if Thomas Flynn is not found in Schuylkill County** in either pli_007 or pli_009. Thomas may have relocated after 1860 to an adjacent county (Carbon, Northumberland, Lebanon, Berks, Columbia). A statewide search filtered by birth year ~1818 and Ireland birthplace will surface plausible candidates and establish whether Thomas died before any official record could have captured his paternity.

---

### Fallback Chains

```
1870: pli_007 (FS) → pli_008 (Ancestry)
1880: pli_009 (FS) → pli_010 (Ancestry) → [if Thomas still missing: pli_013 statewide]
1900: pli_011 (FS) → pli_012 (Ancestry)
```

---

Finding Thomas elsewhere would establish his death window and flag whether he died before Patrick could have reported his parentage to any official record, which bears directly on the evidentiary completeness assessment for q_001.

Now let me conduct a locality survey for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.Good — the locality survey is complete. Now I have everything needed to build the plan. Let me load `research_append` and write it.Now I'll write the full plan in one batched call. The existing plans top out at `pl_001` and items at `pli_003`, so the new plan will be `pl_002` with items `pli_004` through `pli_012`.The plan was written successfully — `pl_002` with nine items (`pli_004`–`pli_012`). Here's the full plan:

---

## Research Plan `pl_002` — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?

**Context:** The 1850 census placed Patrick (age 5, b. Ireland) in the Schuylkill County household of Thomas Flynn (b. ~1818). That co-enumeration is indirect evidence only. Hypothesis `h_001` (Thomas is the father) has no direct support yet. The candidate mother is entirely unnamed. This plan pursues Pennsylvania and Irish sources to establish parentage directly.

---

### Step 1 — `pli_004` · 1860 Census · Schuylkill County · FamilySearch *(free)*
Patrick (~15) should still be living in Thomas Flynn's household, and — critically — **Thomas's wife will be listed by name for the first time**, naming the candidate mother. Co-residence in a second census a decade later also strengthens `h_001`.

### Step 2 — `pli_005` · 1870 Census · Schuylkill County · FamilySearch *(free)*
Continues tracking the household. Thomas's wife again named. Patrick (~25) may still be present or may have recently established his own household nearby — either result is informative.

### Step 3 — `pli_006` · 1880 Census · Schuylkill County · FamilySearch *(free)*
The first census to **explicitly state relationship-to-head**. If Thomas is still alive, any child listed with him has a recorded relationship. Also search for Patrick's own household (age ~35, likely married) — his 1880 record corroborates the immigrant family context.

### Step 4 — `pli_007` · Patrick Flynn Death Certificate, 1908 · FamilySearch *(free)*
Pennsylvania death records begin 1906; Patrick died 1908. The certificate required the informant to supply **the decedent's parents' names and birthplaces** — the highest-probability direct parentage statement in any Pennsylvania record. FamilySearch collection 2255317 is indexed.

### Step 5 — `pli_008` · Patrick Flynn Death Certificate, 1908 · Ancestry *(fallback for pli_007)*
Independent index and image set. Search if FamilySearch returns nothing or the record is unclear.

### Step 6 — `pli_009` · Thomas Flynn Probate · Schuylkill County, ca. 1860–1900 · FamilySearch *(free)*
Thomas (b. ~1818) likely died before 1906, so no official death cert exists. The Schuylkill County Orphans' Court probate records (FamilySearch col. 1417683, image-based) cover this period. **A will naming Patrick as a son is direct evidence of parentage.** Probate will also identify Thomas's widow by name, resolving the unknown mother even if she appears nowhere else.

### Step 7 — `pli_010` · Thomas Flynn Probate · Ancestry *(fallback for pli_009)*
Ancestry's Pennsylvania probate holdings may index records not yet image-linked on FamilySearch. Search if the FamilySearch browse yields nothing.

### Step 8 — `pli_011` · Catholic Church Records (baptisms/marriages) · Schuylkill County, 1845–1890 · FamilySearch + Diocese of Scranton Archives
The Flynn family almost certainly attended a Catholic parish in the coal-mining district (Pottsville, Minersville, St. Clair). **Baptisms of Patrick's younger siblings born in Pennsylvania** would name Thomas Flynn and his wife as parents — direct evidence of the family unit. FamilySearch col. 1401638 covers these; post-1868 registers not yet digitized are held by the Diocese of Scranton Archives.

### Step 9 — `pli_012` · Thomas Flynn + Wife Marriage Record · Ireland, ca. 1838–1845 · IrishGenealogy.ie *(contingent)*
GPS parentage planning requires a dedicated search for the candidate parents' marriage. Because Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845, Thomas and his wife married in Ireland — not Pennsylvania. **This item cannot be executed until the 1860–1880 censuses identify the wife's name.** Once known, search IrishGenealogy.ie (Catholic registers and civil registration) to confirm the marriage and recover her maiden name, which no Pennsylvania source is likely to supply.

---

### Fallback relationships
| If this item yields nothing… | …search this fallback |
|---|---|
| `pli_007` Patrick death cert (FamilySearch) | `pli_008` Ancestry |
| `pli_009` Thomas probate (FamilySearch) | `pli_010` Ancestry |

### Scope
**9 searches** across 4 repositories (FamilySearch, Ancestry, Diocese of Scranton Archives, IrishGenealogy.ie). Steps 1–4 are free and indexed; steps 6 and 8 require browsing image-only collections.

---

Would you like me to **start executing the plan** — beginning with the 1860 census search (`pli_004`)?

entry.rationale: The 1860 census is the single highest-probability source for identifying Thomas Flynn's wife by name. Patrick (~age 15) should still be living in the household, and the enumeration will list the wife as a co-resident — the first chance to name the candidate mother. Co-residence of Patrick in Thomas's household in a second census, a decade after 1850, also strengthens the hypothesis that Thomas is his father. Free access; search first.

The 1870 census continues to track the Thomas Flynn household. Patrick (~age 25) may still be present or may have recently established his own household nearby. Either way, Thomas's wife will again be listed by name, and any remaining children at home corroborate the family unit. If Patrick appears in a separate household, that record establishes his own family context and may name birthplace details useful for cross-referencing church records.

The 1880 census is the first federal enumeration to record the relationship of every person to the head of household. If Thomas Flynn was still alive in 1880 and any children remain at home, their relationship to him is directly stated rather than inferred from household position. Search for both Thomas Flynn's household and Patrick Flynn's own household (he would be ~35 and likely married). Patrick's 1880 household record will also list his wife's birthplace, Ireland or Pennsylvania, corroborating the immigrant family context.

Pennsylvania death certificates began in 1906, and Patrick Flynn died in 1908. His death certificate is the most likely source of direct, explicitly stated parentage information: the informant (typically a spouse or child) was required to supply the decedent's parents' names and birthplaces. The FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968' (col. 2255317) covers this year and is indexed. Even if the informant's knowledge is imperfect, a named father 'Thomas Flynn' from Ireland would corroborate hypothesis h_001 with primary-source authority.

Ancestry holds an independent index and image set for Pennsylvania Death Certificates 1906–1963. If the FamilySearch record (pli_007) is unavailable, unclear, or returns no result under the name searched, Ancestry's independent transcription may surface it — or may supply the actual certificate image where FamilySearch has only an index entry.

If Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) died between roughly 1860 and 1900, his will or letters of administration would be recorded in the Schuylkill County Orphans' Court. A will naming Patrick as a son is direct evidence of parentage; an administration listing heirs is indirect but still significant. The FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994' (col. 1417683) covers Schuylkill County and is image-based — browse by county and estimated death decade. Probate will also identify Thomas's widow by name, supplying the mother's identity even if she is not the informant on a later certificate.

Fallback for the FamilySearch probate search (pli_009). Ancestry's Pennsylvania probate collection may index records not yet image-linked on FamilySearch, or may provide a clearer transcription pathway for a browse-only film. Search if the FamilySearch browse yields no Thomas Flynn probate entry.

Schuylkill County had a large Irish Catholic immigrant population centered on coal-mining towns (Pottsville, Minersville, St. Clair). The Flynn family almost certainly attended a Catholic parish. FamilySearch's 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (col. 1401638) includes Catholic, Lutheran, and Reformed registers. The target records are: (1) baptisms of Patrick's younger siblings born in Pennsylvania, which would name Thomas Flynn and his wife as the parents — establishing the family unit directly; (2) any marriage record for Thomas Flynn and his wife performed in Pennsylvania after their arrival. The Diocese of Scranton Archives (https://dioceseofscranton.org/) holds post-1868 sacramental registers for Schuylkill County parishes not yet digitized; contact if FamilySearch returns nothing. This is the FAN/sibling-cluster search required by BCG topical breadth standards.

GPS parentage planning requires a dedicated plan item for the candidate parents' marriage to each other. Because Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845, Thomas Flynn and his wife almost certainly married in Ireland ca. 1838–1845. The primary online resource is IrishGenealogy.ie (civil registration from 1864; Catholic baptism and marriage registers from many parishes, pre-1900). Once the 1860–1880 censuses identify Thomas's wife's name, her name plus Thomas's name and approximate marriage year can be searched in Irish church records to confirm the marriage and supply her maiden name — which no Pennsylvania source is likely to provide, since the 1850 census does not record it and Pennsylvania civil registration began too late. This item cannot be executed until the wife's name is known; treat it as contingent on census results.

Here's the full plan for **q_001: "Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?"**

---

## Research Plan pl_003 — Parentage of Patrick Flynn

The prior plan (pl_002) is complete. Three sources were searched: the 1860 census, the 1908 death certificate, and Schuylkill County probate on FamilySearch. The proof currently sits at **probable** — all census evidence is *indirect* (relationships not stated in 1850/1860), the death-certificate informant is secondary, and the mother has not been identified at all. This plan targets the remaining gaps.

---

### Step 1 — pli_007 · 1880 Census · FamilySearch
**The single highest-priority item.** The 1880 census is the first to include a relationship-to-head column, meaning it can supply *direct* evidence of a father-son relationship for the first time in this case. Thomas Flynn (~62) and Patrick Flynn (~35) should both appear in Schuylkill County. If Patrick is still in Thomas's household, the relationship is stated explicitly. If Patrick has his own household, Thomas's listing will name his wife and remaining children — crucial for identifying the mother and Patrick's siblings. Free and fully indexed.

### Step 2 — pli_008 · 1870 Census · FamilySearch
Bridges the 1860–1880 gap. Patrick (~25) and Thomas (~52) should both be in Schuylkill County. Three consecutive decennial co-residences (1850, 1860, 1870) would form a strong corroborating pattern. Thomas's 1870 household will also name his wife — the mother still absent from all extracted assertions.

### Step 3 — pli_009 · 1900 Census · FamilySearch
Patrick (~55) is almost certainly heading his own household eight years before his 1908 death. The 1900 census reports birth month and year. Searching Thomas's widow or surviving children in 1900 may identify Patrick's siblings — independent corroboration of the family unit. Free and indexed.

### Step 4 — pli_010 · Thomas Flynn & wife's marriage record · FamilySearch (PA Church Records 1708–1985)
**Mandatory dedicated item for any parentage question.** This record will: **(a)** identify Patrick's mother by maiden name — absent from every record found so far; **(b)** confirm Thomas and his wife as a married couple; **(c)** by its date relative to Patrick's ca. 1845 birth, corroborate Thomas as father (currently identified only by indirect census evidence and a secondary informant on the death certificate). *Limit:* the marriage does not by itself prove Patrick is their child — that link still requires a record placing him with them. PA civil registration didn't start until 1913, so the search targets church registers and county marriage bonds. If the marriage occurred in Ireland before emigration, pli_014 (ship manifest) may supply the wife's name instead.

### Step 5 — pli_011 · Catholic church records, Schuylkill County · FamilySearch (PA Church Records 1708–1985)
Schuylkill County's coal-mining communities were heavily Irish Catholic, served by parishes under the Diocese of Harrisburg (formed 1868 from the Diocese of Philadelphia). Parish registers may include: **(1)** a baptism for Patrick or a sibling naming Thomas and his wife as parents — direct parentage evidence; **(2)** burial records for Thomas Flynn; **(3)** confirmation or other sacramental entries placing family members in the parish. Distinct from pli_010 (the marriage item) — this item targets child and family-event entries.

### Step 6 — pli_012 · Probate · Ancestry *(fallback for pli_006)*
FamilySearch probate search (pli_006, log_006) returned nothing. Ancestry's independent index ("Pennsylvania, Wills and Probate Records, 1683–1993") uses different transcription and coverage. A will naming Patrick as a son would be the strongest possible evidence — direct and from a record Thomas himself created. Date range extended to 1910 to cover uncertainty about Thomas's death year.

### Step 7 — pli_013 · Land/deed records, Schuylkill County · County Courthouse
Grantor-grantee deed books record property transfers. If Thomas Flynn conveyed land to his children (including Patrick) by deed of gift or family settlement, those deeds name the recipients directly — evidence independent of census and vital records. FamilySearch may have browse-only deed images; the county Recorder of Deeds holds the originals. Witnesses and neighbors on Thomas's deeds are also a FAN-cluster lead.

### Step 8 — pli_014 · Immigration / ship manifests · FamilySearch
The family emigrated from Ireland ca. 1844–1849 (Patrick born Ireland ~1845, in Schuylkill County by 1850). A passenger manifest listing Thomas Flynn with a wife and child(ren) would name the mother — the primary target — and, if Patrick appears, constitute direct evidence of the family unit at emigration. FamilySearch indexes lists from Liverpool and Cork; Ancestry has broader coverage and should follow if FamilySearch returns nothing.

---

### Fallback relationship
**Step 6 (Ancestry probate)** is the fallback for pli_006 (FamilySearch probate, already negative).

### Scope
**8 searches** — 5 on FamilySearch (free), 1 on Ancestry (paid), 1 at the county courthouse, 1 spanning FamilySearch/Ancestry.

### Suggested next step
The 1880 census (pli_007) is the place to start — it's free, indexed, and can upgrade the entire case from *probable* to *proved* in a single search. Would you like me to execute that search now?

entry.rationale: The 1880 census is the first federal enumeration to include a relationship-to-head column, making it the single highest-priority untouched source for this question. Thomas Flynn (born ca. 1818) would be approximately 62, and Patrick (born ca. 1845) would be approximately 35. If Thomas is alive and Patrick remains in his household, the 1880 census will provide direct evidence of a father-son relationship — the first direct census evidence in this case, upgrading what the 1850 and 1860 enumerations could only imply indirectly through household position. Even if Patrick had formed his own household by 1880, Thomas's listing will identify his wife and any remaining children, establishing the full family unit and potentially naming Patrick's siblings. Free and fully indexed on FamilySearch.

The 1870 census bridges the gap between the completed 1860 search (pli_004) and the 1880 enumeration (pli_007). Patrick (age ~25) and Thomas (age ~52) should both appear in Schuylkill County. Three consecutive census placements in Thomas's household (1850, 1860, 1870) would constitute a strong pattern of co-residence supporting the parent-child conclusion. Thomas's 1870 household will also name his wife and any children still at home — Patrick's mother has not been identified in any extracted assertion to date.

Patrick Flynn (born ca. 1845) would be approximately 55 in 1900 and almost certainly heading his own household in Schuylkill County, eight years before his 1908 death. The 1900 census reports birth month and year, helping narrow his birth estimate. Searching for Thomas Flynn's widow or surviving children in 1900 may also identify Patrick's siblings, whose existence in the same county would further corroborate shared parentage. Free and indexed on FamilySearch.

A dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn and his wife is required for any parentage question. This record will: (a) identify Patrick's mother by her maiden name — a fact absent from all records searched to date (the 1908 death certificate names Thomas Flynn as father but names no mother; census records were not fully extracted for the wife's identity); (b) confirm Thomas and his wife as a married couple, establishing the family unit; and (c) by its date relative to Patrick's ca. 1845 birth, corroborate that Thomas is the father identified so far only by indirect census evidence and secondary death-certificate testimony. Note the limit: the couple's marriage does not by itself prove Patrick is their child — that link still requires a record placing Patrick with them. Pennsylvania civil marriage registration did not begin until 1913; this item searches church marriage registers and county marriage bonds via FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638). Note: if the marriage occurred in Ireland before emigration and no Pennsylvania record is found, see pli_014 (immigration records) for the wife's name on the ship manifest.

Schuylkill County's coal-mining communities were heavily Irish Catholic. Catholic parishes — including St. Patrick's in Pottsville and surrounding communities — fall under the Diocese of Harrisburg (formed 1868, carved from the Diocese of Philadelphia). Church registers may contain: (1) baptism records for Patrick Flynn or his siblings, naming Thomas and his wife as parents — which would constitute direct evidence of parentage; (2) burial records for Thomas Flynn, helping establish when he died and potentially naming survivors; (3) confirmation or other sacramental records placing family members in the parish. Searched via FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985'). This item is distinct from the parents' marriage record (pli_010) and focuses on child and family-event entries within the parish.

The FamilySearch search for Thomas Flynn's probate record (pli_006, log_006) returned no results. Ancestry maintains an independent index of Pennsylvania probate records ('Pennsylvania, Wills and Probate Records, 1683-1993') with different transcription and coverage. A will naming Patrick as a son would constitute direct evidence of parentage — the strongest possible outcome for this question. The date range is extended to 1910 to account for uncertainty about when Thomas died. If Ancestry also returns nothing, land records (pli_013) and church burial records (pli_011) may help establish Thomas's approximate death year and confirm whether he left an estate.

Grantor-grantee deed indexes at the Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds record property transfers. If Thomas Flynn owned real property and conveyed it to his children — including Patrick — by deed of gift or in family settlement, those deeds would name the children directly, providing evidence of the parent-child relationship independent of census or vital records. FamilySearch may hold browse-only deed images for Schuylkill County; the county recorder holds the originals. Neighbors and witnesses on Thomas Flynn's deeds are also a FAN-cluster lead: associates who appear across multiple deeds may connect to other Flynn family members or help identify the family's parish.

Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845 and was enumerated in Schuylkill County by 1850 at age 5, placing the family's emigration between approximately 1844 and 1849. A ship manifest naming Thomas Flynn with a wife and child(ren) would identify the mother — who has not been named in any record extracted to date — and, if Patrick is listed, constitute direct evidence of the family unit at emigration. Pre-1891 passenger manifests list name, age, sex, and occupation; the wife's name on the manifest is the primary target. FamilySearch indexes Irish passenger lists departing Liverpool and Cork; Ancestry has broader passenger list coverage and should be checked as a second pass if FamilySearch returns nothing.

This record will: (a) identify Patrick's mother by her maiden name — a fact absent from all records searched to date (the 1908 death certificate names Thomas Flynn as father but names no mother; census records were not fully extracted for the wife's identity); (b) confirm Thomas and his wife as a married couple, establishing the family unit; and (c) by its date relative to Patrick's ca. 1845 birth, corroborate that Thomas is the father identified so far only by indirect census evidence and secondary death-certificate testimony. Note the limit: the couple's marriage does not by itself prove Patrick is their child — that link still requires a record placing Patrick with them. Pennsylvania civil marriage registration did not begin until 1913; this item searches church marriage registers and county marriage bonds via FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638). Note: if the marriage occurred in Ireland before emigration and no Pennsylvania record is found, see pli_014 (immigration records) for the wife's name on the ship manifest.

Schuylkill County's coal-mining communities were heavily Irish Catholic. Catholic parishes — including St. Patrick's in Pottsville and surrounding communities — fall under the Diocese of Harrisburg (formed 1868, carved from the Diocese of Philadelphia). Church registers may contain: (1) baptism records for Patrick Flynn or his siblings, naming Thomas and his wife as parents — which would constitute direct evidence of parentage; (2) burial records for Thomas Flynn, helping establish when he died and potentially naming survivors; (3) confirmation or other sacramental records placing family members in the parish. Searched via FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985'). This item is distinct from the parents' marriage record (pli_010) and focuses on child and family-event entries within the parish.

The FamilySearch search for Thomas Flynn's probate record (pli_006, log_006) returned no results. Ancestry maintains an independent index of Pennsylvania probate records ('Pennsylvania, Wills and Probate Records, 1683-1993') with different transcription and coverage. A will naming Patrick as a son would constitute direct evidence of parentage — the strongest possible outcome for this question. The date range is extended to 1910 to account for uncertainty about when Thomas died. If Ancestry also returns nothing, land records (pli_013) and church burial records (pli_011) may help establish Thomas's approximate death year and confirm whether he left an estate.

Grantor-grantee deed indexes at the Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds record property transfers. If Thomas Flynn owned real property and conveyed it to his children — including Patrick — by deed of gift or in family settlement, those deeds would name the children directly, providing evidence of the parent-child relationship independent of census or vital records. FamilySearch may hold browse-only deed images for Schuylkill County; the county recorder holds the originals. Neighbors and witnesses on Thomas Flynn's deeds are also a FAN-cluster lead: associates who appear across multiple deeds may connect to other Flynn family members or help identify the family's parish.

Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845 and was enumerated in Schuylkill County by 1850 at age 5, placing the family's emigration between approximately 1844 and 1849. A ship manifest naming Thomas Flynn with a wife and child(ren) would identify the mother — who has not been named in any record extracted to date — and, if Patrick is listed, constitute direct evidence of the family unit at emigration. Pre-1891 passenger manifests list name, age, sex, and occupation; the wife's name on the manifest is the primary target. FamilySearch indexes Irish passenger lists departing Liverpool and Cork; Ancestry has broader passenger list coverage and should be checked as a second pass if FamilySearch returns nothing.

Good. Now I have the full picture. Let me conduct the locality survey in parallel — place lookup, FamilySearch collections for Denmark, external links, wiki guidance, and volume search.Good. I have everything needed. Let me now write the plan in a single batched `research_append` call — plan shell plus 7 items. No existing plans, so it'll be `pl_001` and items `pli_001`–`pli_007`.**pl_001** is written — 7 items, all `planned`. Here's the full plan:

---

## Research Plan: pl_001
**Question q_001 —** Who was the father of Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg (b. 20 Aug 1779, Stagstrup Sogn, Thisted Amt, Denmark)?

**Starting-point note:** The patronymic "Nielsen" establishes only that the father's given name was **Niels** — no father is documented in the tree and no records have been searched yet. All items below treat the father's identity as unknown until evidence is found.

---

### Items in execution order

| # | Record type | Jurisdiction | Period | Repository |
|---|------------|-------------|--------|------------|
| pli_001 | Church (baptism) | Stagstrup, Thisted | 1779 | FamilySearch |
| pli_002 | Church (baptism) — *fallback for pli_001* | Stagstrup, Thisted | 1779 | Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline |
| pli_003 | Census | Stagstrup, Thisted | 1787 | FamilySearch |
| pli_004 | Military — lægdsruller | Thisted Amt | 1789–1810 | FamilySearch / Rigsarkivet |
| pli_005 | Church (parents' marriage) | Stagstrup, Thisted | c. 1765–1778 | FamilySearch |
| pli_006 | Probate — skifteprotokol | Thisted Amt (herred TBD) | 1780–1830 | FamilySearch |
| pli_007 | Census (FAN) — *fallback for pli_003* | Stagstrup, Thisted | 1801 | FamilySearch |

---

### Rationales

**pli_001 — Mikkel's 1779 baptism (FamilySearch)**
The Stagstrup kirkebog baptism entry should name the father directly — the single highest-probability item. Collection 2078555; volume 004130616 covers Stagstrup 1763–1814 but is only **3% indexed** — start with an indexed name search, then browse the image volume to the August 1779 section.

**pli_002 — Baptism fallback (Rigsarkivet)**
Rigsarkivet *arkivalieronline* (sa.dk) hosts free browsable images of Thisted Amt church books including Stagstrup. Use if the FamilySearch image is unclear or unavailable.

**pli_003 — 1787 Danish census (FamilySearch)**
Collection 1401625 is fully indexed. Mikkel (~8 years old) should appear in his father's household, directly identifying the father as head of household.

**pli_004 — Military levying rolls / lægdsruller (FamilySearch + Rigsarkivet)**
The *lægdsruller* enroll boys from birth or early childhood **under the father's name, written directly above the son's entry** — this is *direct* parentage evidence, not a fallback. Mikkel would be enrolled in the Thisted Amt district from ~1789. Collection 2513529; volume 004748896 covers Thisted Amt 1789–1814 and is **0% indexed** (browse-only). Also free at Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline.

**pli_005 — Parents' marriage (FamilySearch)**
A dedicated search for the marriage of "Niels [surname]" to Mikkel's mother, circa 1765–1778, in the Stagstrup register. Supplies the mother's maiden name; corroborates the father by date. *Does not by itself place Mikkel as their child* — that link requires pli_001 or pli_003. Same volume (004130616) as pli_001.

**pli_006 — Father's probate / skifteprotokol (FamilySearch)**
The father's estate settlement would name Mikkel as an heir — strong corroborating parentage evidence. Collection 2015318; volume 004392710 covers Hassing Herred 1690–1850 (browse-only). **Verify which herred covers Stagstrup** before searching — Thisted Amt had several herreder (Hillerslev, Hassing, Hundborg, Refs) with separate records.

**pli_007 — 1801 census / FAN (FamilySearch)**
FAN cluster and fallback for pli_003. Mikkel is ~22 in 1801 and may still be in the father's household or nearby. Also scan Stagstrup for all "Niels [surname]" household heads as candidates for the father or paternal relatives.

---

### Fallback relationships
- pli_001 fails → pli_002 (Rigsarkivet images)
- pli_003 fails → pli_007 (1801 census)

### Scope
7 searches. Several items (pli_001, pli_004, pli_006) require browsing image volumes rather than indexed search — budget extra time for those.

---

**Ready to start?** Say "search for Mikkel's baptism" to execute pli_001, or "run the plan" to hand off to `search-records`.

## Research Plan Review — Q_001: Parents of Patrick Flynn

**Objective:** Identify the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland/PA; d. 1908, Schuylkill County, PA)

| 2 | `pli_005` | 1908 Death Certificate | FamilySearch | ✅ Completed |
| 3 | `pli_006` | Thomas Flynn Probate/Will, 1870–1890 | FamilySearch | 🔄 In Progress |

---

### What's Been Found So Far

- **1850 Census** — Patrick Flynn (age 5) in Thomas Flynn household, Schuylkill Co. *(indirect evidence of parentage — relationship not stated)*
- **1860 Census** — Patrick Flynn (age 15) again in Thomas Flynn household *(indirect — relationship column not introduced until 1880)*
- **1908 Death Certificate** — Names Thomas Flynn as father *(direct evidence, but secondary informant — son-in-law James Brown)*

A **Thomas Flynn will dated 1881** has already been located (`src_006`, FamilySearch ARK `3:1:3Q9M-CS4V-7TXW`), but **no assertions have been extracted from it yet** and `pli_006` remains `in_progress`.

---

---

## ✅ Confirmed Next Step

The will has been located but not yet processed. The immediate action is to run **record extraction** on that source to pull out assertions bearing on `q_001`. Specifically, the will may:
- Explicitly name Patrick Flynn as a son (which would provide **direct, primary evidence** of parentage)
- Name a wife/mother of Patrick (potentially identifying the maternal line)

If the will names Patrick as a son, the parentage hypothesis (`h_001`) advances from **Probable → Proved**, and `q_001` can move toward resolution.

## Research Plan pl_002 — q_001: Who were Patrick Flynn's parents?

### ⚑ BCG Standard 11 — Starting-point flag

The tree already holds three sources supporting the Thomas→Patrick relationship (R1) that **have not been formally logged or extracted** into research.json:
- **S2** — 1860 census, Schuylkill Co., dwelling 112
- **S3** — Pennsylvania death certificate no. 4521 (12 Mar 1908)
- **S4** — Schuylkill County Probate Records

Items 1–4 below are targeted at these records. They need formal search-records / record-extraction passes before they can anchor a proof.

---

### The plan — in execution order

| # | ID | Record type | Jurisdiction | Date range | Repository | Notes |
|---|----|-------------|-------------|------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | pli_004 | Vital record | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1906–1910 | FamilySearch | PA death cert (cert. no. 4521, S3 in tree — needs extraction). May directly name both parents. |
| 2 | pli_005 | Vital record | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1906–1910 | Ancestry | *Fallback for pli_004* — independent index if FS spelling variant misses it. |
| 3 | pli_006 | Census | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1860 | FamilySearch | 1860 census, dwelling 112 (S2 in tree — needs extraction). Patrick ~15, likely still in Thomas's household; may name mother. |
| 4 | pli_007 | Probate | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1860–1908 | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn will/administration (S4 in tree — needs extraction). A will naming Patrick is direct parentage evidence. |
| 5 | pli_008 | Church | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1835–1850 | FamilySearch | **Mandatory parentage item** — Thomas Flynn's marriage. Establishes the mother's maiden name (unavailable from any census or death record). Confirms couple predates Patrick's ~1845 birth. Collection 1401638. |
| 6 | pli_009 | Church | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1845–1875 | FamilySearch | Catholic baptisms of Flynn children born in PA. Each sibling's baptism names Thomas and his wife as parents — multiple independent corroborations. Also satisfies BCG Standard 14 FAN requirement. Collection 1401638. |
| 7 | pli_010 | Church | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1845–1875 | Other (Diocese of Scranton Archives) | *Fallback for pli_009* — diocesan original registers not fully digitized on FS. |
| 8 | pli_011 | Census | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1880 | FamilySearch | First census with relationship-to-head column — can directly state "son" or "father." Thomas ~62, Patrick ~35. |
| 9 | pli_012 | Census | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1870 | FamilySearch | Third decade of household evidence. Thomas ~52, Patrick ~25. Identifies the wife/mother if she's present. |
| 10 | pli_013 | Newspaper | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1908 | Other (Miners' Journal / HSSC) | Patrick's 1908 obituary. May name parents, Irish origin, or siblings. |
| 11 | pli_014 | Immigration | Pennsylvania | 1840–1875 | Ancestry | Thomas Flynn naturalization (PA Naturalization Records, 1794–1931). Identifies Thomas's Irish county of origin; also the key FAN-cluster search. |

### Fallback relationships
- **pli_005** backs up pli_004 (death cert, Ancestry if FS misses it)
- **pli_010** backs up pli_009 (Diocese of Scranton if FamilySearch church collection is incomplete)

### Scope
**11 searches** across FamilySearch (7), Ancestry (2), and physical/contact repositories (2).

---

### What this plan is designed to prove

The current evidence — one 1850 census co-enumeration — is indirect and stands at *possible* (ps_001). This plan attacks the question from four independent angles:

1. **Direct statement of parentage** — death certificate (pli_004/005) and probate will (pli_007)
2. **Continued household co-residence** — 1860 (pli_006), 1870 (pli_012), and 1880 (pli_011) censuses
3. **The mother's identity** — Thomas's marriage record (pli_008) and sibling baptisms (pli_009/010)
4. **Contextual and FAN** — obituary (pli_013) and Thomas's naturalization (pli_014)

---

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County (tree F2). Pennsylvania mandated death certificates from 1 January 1906, so a certificate almost certainly exists. The tree already references this record as source S3 ('Pennsylvania Death Certificates, cert. no. 4521') supporting the Thomas–Patrick parent-child relationship (R1), but it has not been formally logged or extracted into research.json. Death certificates of this era name the deceased's birthplace and parents' names and birthplaces — potentially the only surviving record that directly states both of Patrick's parents by name. FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968' (collection 2255317) is fully indexed. BCG Standard 11: this source is in the tree but unextracted; formally searching and extracting it is the plan's first priority.

Ancestry independently indexed the same Pennsylvania death certificate collection (1906–1963). If FamilySearch's index does not surface cert. no. 4521 under the expected name form — due to transcription variation in 'Flynn' or the given name — Ancestry's independent index provides a second access path to the same underlying record. Execute only if pli_004 yields no match.

The tree references source S2 ('1860 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill Co., dwelling 112') as supporting the Thomas–Patrick relationship (R1), but this record has not been formally logged or extracted in research.json. Patrick would be ~15 in 1860 and is most likely still in Thomas's household. The 1860 census enumerates the full household and may name Patrick's mother for the first time. Formally extracting this record advances the parentage evidence from one to two decades of documented co-residence and produces the first identified candidate for Patrick's mother.

The tree references source S4 ('Schuylkill County Probate Records') as supporting the Thomas–Patrick parent-child relationship (R1), but no log entries or assertions for this record appear in research.json. Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) almost certainly died before Patrick's 1908 death. A will or administration naming Patrick Flynn as a son would be direct evidence of parentage; a guardian's account or intestate distribution would name the widow (Patrick's mother). FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994' (collection 1417683) holds Schuylkill County probate images. BCG Standard 11: this record is referenced in the tree but unextracted and must be formally verified.

A dedicated search for Thomas Flynn's marriage to Patrick's mother is mandatory in every parentage plan. The marriage record: (a) confirms Thomas and his wife as a married couple predating Patrick's ~1845 birth; (b) supplies the mother's maiden name, which census and death records typically omit; (c) by its date relative to ~1845, corroborates Thomas as father — the couple must have been wed before Patrick was born. As Irish Catholic immigrants in Schuylkill County's anthracite coal community, the marriage is most likely in a Catholic parish register (Diocese of Philadelphia before 1868). FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (collection 1401638) is the primary indexed source. Note: this record proves Thomas and his wife married, not that Patrick is their child — that link still requires a record placing Patrick with them (already supplied by the 1850 and 1860 censuses).

Schuylkill County was home to one of Pennsylvania's largest Irish Catholic communities, driven by anthracite coal mining. If Thomas and his wife had children born in Pennsylvania after 1845, their Catholic baptismal records would name both parents directly — direct evidence linking Thomas Flynn and his wife as parents of Patrick's siblings, and by extension corroborating the family unit. Searching FamilySearch collection 1401638 for Flynn baptisms in Schuylkill County 1845–1875 may identify siblings, each baptism being an independent record naming the parents. This also fulfills the BCG Standard 14 FAN requirement — Patrick's siblings are his closest associates.

The FamilySearch church collection (1401638) does not contain all Schuylkill County Catholic parish registers. The Diocese of Scranton Archives holds original sacramental registers for Schuylkill County parishes from 1868 onward, and earlier Philadelphia diocesan materials for some congregations. If pli_010 (FamilySearch church records) yields no relevant Flynn baptisms or marriage record, contact the Diocese of Scranton Archives directly. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County may also hold microfilm copies of local parish registers.

The 1880 federal census is the first to record each household member's relationship to the head — the first census capable of directly stating a parent-child relationship. Thomas Flynn (~62 in 1880) may still be living with or near his wife; any children still in the household would have explicit relationship designations. Even if Patrick (~35) has his own household, the Thomas Flynn household in 1880 names the mother and surviving children with stated relationships, providing the strongest census evidence for family composition.

No 1870 census search has been logged for this project. Patrick would be ~25 in 1870 and may still be in Thomas's household or living nearby. Thomas (~52) and his wife may be enumerated together in 1870, identifying the mother and establishing a third decade of household evidence. Even if Patrick has struck out on his own, finding the Thomas Flynn household in 1870 confirms the family unit's continued presence in Schuylkill County between the confirmed 1860 co-residence and Thomas's eventual death.

Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County. Obituaries in local papers (Miners' Journal, Pottsville Republican) of this era frequently named parents, place of Irish origin, surviving family members, and sometimes siblings. An obituary could directly name Patrick's parents or confirm him as Thomas Flynn's son. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County holds newspaper collections including the Miners' Journal; Newspapers.com and Chronicling America may also index 1908 Schuylkill County papers.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland before 1850. Pennsylvania naturalizations before 1906 occurred in county courts (Quarter Sessions, Common Pleas); the prothonotary's declaration of intent and oath sometimes include age, occupation, and county of origin. Even if parentage is not stated, identifying Thomas's Irish county of origin could enable a search for the Flynn baptism in Ireland that would directly name Patrick's parents. Ancestry 'Pennsylvania, U.S., Naturalization Records, 1794–1931' is the primary indexed source. Thomas Flynn as presumptive father is also the key FAN-cluster associate per BCG Standard 14.

FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968' (collection 2255317) is fully indexed. BCG Standard 11: this source is in the tree but unextracted; formally searching and extracting it is the plan's first priority.

Ancestry independently indexed the same Pennsylvania death certificate collection (1906–1963). If FamilySearch's index does not surface cert. no. 4521 under the expected name form — due to transcription variation in 'Flynn' or the given name — Ancestry's independent index provides a second access path to the same underlying record. Execute only if pli_004 yields no match.

The tree references source S2 ('1860 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill Co., dwelling 112') as supporting the Thomas–Patrick relationship (R1), but this record has not been formally logged or extracted in research.json. Patrick would be ~15 in 1860 and is most likely still in Thomas's household. The 1860 census enumerates the full household and may name Patrick's mother for the first time. Formally extracting this record advances the parentage evidence from one to two decades of documented co-residence and produces the first identified candidate for Patrick's mother.

The tree references source S4 ('Schuylkill County Probate Records') as supporting the Thomas–Patrick parent-child relationship (R1), but no log entries or assertions for this record appear in research.json. Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) almost certainly died before Patrick's 1908 death. A will or administration naming Patrick Flynn as a son would be direct evidence of parentage; a guardian's account or intestate distribution would name the widow (Patrick's mother). FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994' (collection 1417683) holds Schuylkill County probate images. BCG Standard 11: this record is referenced in the tree but unextracted and must be formally verified.

A dedicated search for Thomas Flynn's marriage to Patrick's mother is mandatory in every parentage plan. The marriage record: (a) confirms Thomas and his wife as a married couple predating Patrick's ~1845 birth; (b) supplies the mother's maiden name, which census and death records typically omit; (c) by its date relative to ~1845, corroborates Thomas as father — the couple must have been wed before Patrick was born. As Irish Catholic immigrants in Schuylkill County's anthracite coal community, the marriage is most likely in a Catholic parish register (Diocese of Philadelphia before 1868). FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (collection 1401638) is the primary indexed source. Note: this record proves Thomas and his wife married, not that Patrick is their child — that link still requires a record placing Patrick with them (already supplied by the 1850 and 1860 censuses).

Schuylkill County was home to one of Pennsylvania's largest Irish Catholic communities, driven by anthracite coal mining. If Thomas and his wife had children born in Pennsylvania after 1845, their Catholic baptismal records would name both parents directly — direct evidence linking Thomas Flynn and his wife as parents of Patrick's siblings, and by extension corroborating the family unit. Searching FamilySearch collection 1401638 for Flynn baptisms in Schuylkill County 1845–1875 may identify siblings, each baptism being an independent record naming the parents. This also fulfills the BCG Standard 14 FAN requirement — Patrick's siblings are his closest associates.

The FamilySearch church collection (1401638) does not contain all Schuylkill County Catholic parish registers. The Diocese of Scranton Archives holds original sacramental registers for Schuylkill County parishes from 1868 onward, and earlier Philadelphia diocesan materials for some congregations. If pli_010 (FamilySearch church records) yields no relevant Flynn baptisms or marriage record, contact the Diocese of Scranton Archives directly. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County may also hold microfilm copies of local parish registers.

The 1880 federal census is the first to record each household member's relationship to the head — the first census capable of directly stating a parent-child relationship. Thomas Flynn (~62 in 1880) may still be living with or near his wife; any children still in the household would have explicit relationship designations. Even if Patrick (~35) has his own household, the Thomas Flynn household in 1880 names the mother and surviving children with stated relationships, providing the strongest census evidence for family composition.

No 1870 census search has been logged for this project. Patrick would be ~25 in 1870 and may still be in Thomas's household or living nearby. Thomas (~52) and his wife may be enumerated together in 1870, identifying the mother and establishing a third decade of household evidence. Even if Patrick has struck out on his own, finding the Thomas Flynn household in 1870 confirms the family unit's continued presence in Schuylkill County between the confirmed 1860 co-residence and Thomas's eventual death.

Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County. Obituaries in local papers (Miners' Journal, Pottsville Republican) of this era frequently named parents, place of Irish origin, surviving family members, and sometimes siblings. An obituary could directly name Patrick's parents or confirm him as Thomas Flynn's son. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County holds newspaper collections including the Miners' Journal; Newspapers.com and Chronicling America may also index 1908 Schuylkill County papers.

Thomas Flynn immigrated from Ireland before 1850. Pennsylvania naturalizations before 1906 occurred in county courts (Quarter Sessions, Common Pleas); the prothonotary's declaration of intent and oath sometimes include age, occupation, and county of origin. Even if parentage is not stated, identifying Thomas's Irish county of origin could enable a search for the Flynn baptism in Ireland that would directly name Patrick's parents. Ancestry 'Pennsylvania, U.S., Naturalization Records, 1794–1931' is the primary indexed source. Thomas Flynn as presumptive father is also the key FAN-cluster associate per BCG Standard 14.

Here's the current research plan for the parentage question:

---

## Research Plan: pl_002
**Question (q_001):** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?
**Plan status:** Active | **Created:** 2 May 2026

---

### Plan Items

| # | Record Type | Jurisdiction | Period | Repository | Status |
|---|------------|-------------|--------|-----------|--------|
| pli_004 | Census | Schuylkill County, PA | 1860 | FamilySearch | ✅ Completed |
| pli_005 | Vital record (death certificate) | Schuylkill County, PA | 1908 | FamilySearch | ✅ Completed |
| pli_006 | Probate | Schuylkill County, PA | 1870–1890 | FamilySearch | 🔄 In Progress |

---

### What's Been Found

- **pli_004 (1860 census):** Patrick Flynn, age 15, confirmed in Thomas Flynn's household — indirect evidence of parentage (relationship column didn't exist until 1880).
- **pli_005 (death certificate):** Patrick's 1908 death certificate names **Thomas Flynn** as his father, reported by son-in-law James Brown (secondary informant).

These two results, combined with the earlier 1850 census work (pl_001), support a **probable** conclusion that Thomas Flynn was Patrick's father (proof summary ps_001, `tier: probable`).

---

### Next Step

**pli_006** — the Thomas Flynn probate search on FamilySearch — is currently **in progress**. This is the most important remaining item: a will explicitly naming Patrick as a son would be *direct evidence from a primary informant*, which would upgrade the conclusion from **probable** to **proved**.

The target period is 1870–1890 (Thomas Flynn presumably died sometime during that window, having last appeared in the 1860 census with Patrick).

---

The proof summary itself flags that the **1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses** haven't been searched. These aren't currently in the plan — once pli_006 is resolved, you may want to extend the plan (or add a new plan) to cover those census years, particularly to corroborate the parent-child relationship across more enumeration years and close the 1860–1908 timeline gap.

Would you like to start executing the probate search (pli_006), or extend the plan first to add the missing census years?

**"Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845 PA, d. 1908)?"**

- All other items run independently in sequence.

entry.rationale: A newly found reference suggests Thomas Flynn may have died ca. 1865, moving the death window a decade earlier than the 1870-1890 range targeted in superseded pl_002. Schuylkill County probate records covering this period are available on FamilySearch. A will or letters of administration naming Patrick as a son would supply direct evidence of parentage. Highest-probability, free-access source — search first.

Fallback for pli_007 if FamilySearch returns nothing. Ancestry indexes Pennsylvania probate records independently and may surface wills or administrations not yet indexed or fully digitized on FamilySearch. Same date range (1860-1870) per the revised death estimate.

Two related searches: (1) 1870 population census — Thomas Flynn's absence from Schuylkill County in 1870 (after confirmed presence in 1860) would establish death before June 1870, corroborating the ca. 1865 estimate; his presence would rule out a pre-1870 death. (2) 1870 mortality schedule — lists every Schuylkill County resident who died in the 12 months before the June 1870 census (June 1869–June 1870); Thomas would appear here if he died in that specific window. Both schedules are indexed on FamilySearch.

Pennsylvania county tax assessments were compiled annually from taxable inhabitants. Tracking Thomas Flynn year-by-year through these rolls can narrow his death to a specific year within the 1860-1870 window — the year he drops off (or when his widow or estate appears) brackets the death date. Particularly useful given that probate records are often filed 1-2 years after death. FamilySearch has digitized many Pennsylvania county tax records.

A ca. 1865 death in the coal region raises the possibility of Civil War service. Thomas would have been in his mid-40s in 1861 (born ~1818, per 1850 census), older but not outside the eligible range for Pennsylvania regiments. Compiled Military Service Records (CMSR) and pension files on FamilySearch and Fold3 may document death in service. A widow's pension application by Thomas's wife would name their children — including Patrick — providing direct parentage evidence as a bonus.

Patrick Flynn's baptismal record was found at St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville — the family's likely parish. Burial registers for St. Mary's and other Schuylkill County Catholic parishes for 1860-1870 may record Thomas Flynn's interment, naming survivors or next of kin. Church burial records are especially valuable for the pre-1906 period before Pennsylvania required death registration. FamilySearch holds digitized images of many Pennsylvania Catholic registers.

Church burial records are especially valuable for the pre-1906 period before Pennsylvania required death registration. FamilySearch holds digitized images of many Pennsylvania Catholic registers.
